				× 7.6% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How redesigning the main donate button on the homepage affected traffic at year-end

rows.name: New - Green Donate to TGC Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the main donation button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2021
Leading up to calendar year-end, we know that visitors to the TGC homepage had a high motivation to give. We wanted to make sure that potentially highly motivated donors could easily find where to make their year-end gift.
We rolled out a new button design on the homepage to see if we could make it easier for potential donors to find the donation page, but we wanted to run this as a test against the old version to capture the impact.
The new button (running as the control for this experiment) read “Donate to TGC” and we made it green to stand out for visual emphasis while the old button (the treatment version) simply said “Give” and visually matched all other menu options in the header.
We split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the new button (green button) drove more traffic to the donation page. Of these two buttons, the “Donate to TGC” button provided the additional clarity necessary to increase traffic to the page while the white "Give" button saw a decrease in traffic of 20%.
Because we were running a separate test on the donation page, we did not factor in conversion rate or revenue on the donation page from this experiment. We only wanted to measure the impact on clicks through to the donation page. What we learned is that the more visual button made it easier for potential donors to find the next step we want them to take by adding clarity in the messaging and visually leading them where to go.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
25.3% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2022 - 11/25/2022
When launching a new content offer, we had similar donation page language in place from previous content offers and we knew that the housefile visitors would likely be returning visitors. We wanted to test a new approach that condensed some lengthy copy on the treatment into 3 specific items the reader should know. We also updated the headline from “Thank you” to “Congratulations” and mentioned the title of the specific offer they had requested. We split traffic equally between the two pages and ran the experiment for 2 weeks before evaluating results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After two weeks, we saw a 25.7% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence, so the results were indicating a directional decrease. We believe the treatment language in the headline "you're all set" was causing visitors to leave the page before reading the remaining message. We saw valid decreases for many visitor segments, so we decided to end the experiment early and reassess the best treatment to run for these visitors. On future testing for this page, we will reconstruct the headline language that asks people to read the message below before they exit the site. Then, we can better evaluate if the "3 things you should know" approach was the true cause for the decrease, or if there was another element from the radical redesign (in this case, the headline) that was inadvertently causing friction that interfered with the test and prevented people consuming the message.

experiments.title: How offering a free ebook for email acquisition in the home page footer instead of a general email subscription form impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook as an email acquisition method in the home page footer for page vistiors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2022 - 10/13/2022
In their home page footer, TGC gives users the opportunity to subscribe to email updates. The section says “Email Updates” and users can enter their email below and sign up. We wondered if offering a free ebook to users when they sign up would acquire more emails than the existing “email updates” sign-up opportunity, so we tested the idea. In the footer, we replaced the email updates section with an offer to receive a popular ebook for free. We included value prop telling the user how the ebook could help them and below was a sign up form including first name, last name, and email, and a button that read “Get my free ebook »”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control saw a 0.03% conversion rate compared to a 0.02% conversion rate for the treatment. Ultimately, the treatment had a 26.6% decrease in emails acquired with an 89.1% level of confidence. Users were more likely to sign up for email updates than to receive a free ebook.

Given that TGC offers a ton of content, which users can see as they scroll through the home page and down to the footer of the page, a user's primary intent may be to receive email updates for new content and new articles. So the opportunity to receive only a single free ebook is limiting compared to receiving lots of updates with lots of new content.

We're going to run this same experiment on TGC's Commentaries page to see if/how the user motivation adjusts from the home page to the commentaries page.

It may be a good idea to test adding value prop language to the home page footer in line with receiving email updates. Right now, the sign up opportunity only says "Email Updates" offering no value prop as to why the user might want to sign up for email updates.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: How tightening copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink affected click rates on an auto-responder
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tightening the copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/15/2022
The Gospel Coalition developed a content offer which provided a free e-book download when people signed up. To further cultivate this audience and provide even greater value to their experience, a discussion guide was sent the next day to anyone who downloaded the ebook. We wanted to know how different tones and link presentations might affect click rates and developed a treatment which would be run through an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 9.2% directional lift in click rates with an 87.2% level of confidence indicating that the shorter, more direct copy of our treatment (compared to the more conversational, interrogative copy of the control) did have a measurable positive effect on click rates.
More subtly, the treatment copy was structured to provide the "why" (value proposition) of downloading the question guide at the beginning of the email, directly after the salutation. This is based on the theory of influence that states that providing a clear 'why' within a request is more likely to trigger a response of compliance—in this case by clicking to download the question guide "to help you get the most from this insightful resource."
This value proposition is later reinforced near the CTA (call to action) by illustrating that the question guide will a) help readers apply the text's lessons to their own life and b) provide a framework for discussing the text with others.
The treatment also hypothesized that a simple hyperlink may also help improve click rates over a button which, while very visible, may create additional friction at the CTA.
These results show that providing a "why" and streamlining the language within an email promotion can trigger a more favorable response from recipients by creating a slippery slide which carries them effortlessly from salutation to CTA while providing enough benefit for recipients to click through.
In the future, we will test these elements individually to help isolate the variable that produced the lift.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition impacted email signups for a newsletter – Part 1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal and clarity for a weekly newsletter offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 03/27/2022
In looking at ways to acquire more email addresses through a weekly newsletter offer, we looked at the current value prop offered on the landing page. With just a brief headline on the control, we saw an opportunity to develop a treatment that would provide additional language to increase specificity and exclusivity in order to get more people to subscribe. We developed a version with more appeal, outlining the benefits to the reader and giving them more details about the newsletter’s contents. We split email traffic to this page from the new subscriber welcome series.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 3 weeks, we observed a directional decrease of 19.9% in email acquisition with a 92% level of confidence. Specifically, we saw valid decreases for desktop visitors and email visitors. Because we had already "made the sale" in the body copy of the email where our visitors were arriving from for why someone should subscribe, we believe the additional value proposition was actually causing unnecessary friction on the page. We would like to run a similar test in the future to see how this would impact visitors from other channels or calls to action in other website placements that do not lead with clarity and value proposition like the email experience provides.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to sticky bar messaging impacted donations for a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and seasonally relevant messaging for website visitors will achieve more calendar year-end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2021 - 01/04/2022
During a calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test various messaging on a sticky bar to see what would most resonate with donors to get them to the donation page to make a gift. We had a general control message that prompted a gift to ‘share resources with believers’ and mentioned the upcoming deadline. The first treatment added clarity around what the donor’s gift would accomplish – highlighting that each dollar would impact 10 people. The second treatment posed a question to introduce donor autonomy – will you help us? The third treatment was the same message, but appeared at the bottom of the screen rather than the top of the page. We split traffic to all 4 versions for site visitors during the last week of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach statistical significance since the experiment was time-bound and only ran for a short period of time, what we did find is that the language that clearly outlined the impact of each dollar given seemed to be moving more donors to conversion. This will need to be tested again outside of a high urgency campaign window for more clear results, but we can find additional ways to incorporate this language into the value proposition for future donor communications.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 5.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 163.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 78.0% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 5.8% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How structuring the donation page copy in a user-friendly manner impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that structuring the donation page copy in a user-friendly manner and spanning the value prop across “three important things” for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/28/2022
TGC’s Digital Discipleship eBook funnel was producing a low instant conversion rate. Users that reached the instant donation page after signing up to receive the eBook were making donations at a rate well below the benchmark. We came across a TGC video that we thought did an excellent job of highlighting our cause and what we’re hoping to accomplish. So we transcribed it, and transferred the three important things stated in the video into the instant donation page copy for this offer. We wondered if diverting from the traditional copy format on the donation page and structuring the copy in a more user-friendly manner would increase donor conversion rate.
Thus, we updated the headline to invoke more excitement toward the step that the user just took. We then implemented the three important things from the video into the donation page copy, numbering them so they would be easy for the user to follow.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test resulted in zero donations for the control and treatment variants, so there was no learning. The Digital Discipleship eBook offer was not performing well at the ad level either, so we turned off the ad campaign we had running for it in Facebook. As a result, there was little web traffic actually getting to the instant donation page for the offer. This test may be worth running again if we can optimize the campaign at the ad level and generate more users to the instant donation page.

experiments.title: How emphasizing copy and expanding the value proposition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing key sentences within the copy and offering real-life application through the copy for page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 10/28/2022
TGC’s Secular Creed eBook funnel was lacking in instant donor conversion rate. Despite having a high sign-up rate for the offer, those who signed up were not converting at the end of the funnel by not donating through the instant donation page. We wondered if the subpar results were due to a lack of motivation. Therefore, we developed a copy treatment that expanded the value proposition, adding real-life application by talking about how resources like this one will help you resolve conflict and have impactful conversions with friends, neighbors, coworkers, and family members. We also bolded key sentences to get the message across in a stronger fashion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were inconclusive. Both the control and treatment led to 5 donations, and the treatment ultimately had just a 0.70% lift and only a 0.88% level of confidence. Potential donors gave little indication as to which donation page copy most resonated with them.

It's also difficult to know which components of the treatment helped or hurt donor conversion rate, as there were too many differences between the control and the treatment. The treatment had a longer headline, inconsistent bolding, and different copy overall. We may want to isolate these elements in future tests on this donation page.

experiments.title: How adding images of associated premiums impacted conversion rate and recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that putting the ebook premiums on display for potential donors to physically see will achieve an increase in donations and specifically recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 10/01/2022
TGC has a recurring giving funnel in which they offer premiums as an incentive for someone making a recurring gift. The premiums are clearly stated in the copy, however, there were no images of the premiums on the page. We thought that adding images of the premiums would perhaps increase donations and recurring donations as users would be able to see the premiums that they would be receiving when making a recurring donation. We added the images after mentioning the premium incentive and above the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcomes of the experiment were negligible. Neither the control nor the treatment led to a recurring donation, and the treatment resulted in a 20.5% increase in one-time donations (3 donations vs. 2) but with only a 16.5% level of confidence. The addition of the premium images carried led to a small and invalid impact.

We may want to push the experiment a step further by adding some sort of preview of the ebooks. This could consist of an image and a summary of the ebook (on-page) since we're trying to stress the premiums as an incentive to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned landing page impacted email acquisition for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity and credibility through imagery for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in online course subscribers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/21/2022
On a past eBook offer, we had seen a highly visual landing page increase email conversion so we wanted to test the same hypothesis on an online course offer. We developed a treatment that highlighted an image of Tim Keller, one of the instructors featured in the course, to add some clarity and credibility to the offer. We split traffic between the plain-text control vs. the radically redesigned layout of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw there was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rate with just a 23.3% level of confidence so the test is not valid and the results were flat. One interesting thing to note is when we looked downstream one step further into the funnel, donations experienced a 68.2% decrease with a 86% level of confidence in the treatment, indicating a directional decline. We believe this is because the highly designed landing page doesn't have enough synergy with the plain-text donation page that follows it, making the control landing page more congruent with the experience for each step. Next, we will test adding the Faith & Work course graphic on the plain-text page to see if adding a visual element will help indicate to visitors they are taking the correct next step to get the offer and increase email acquisition.

experiments.title: How aligning with a donor’s beliefs impacted conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing donor alignment and donor autonomy for post-survey donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 03/27/2022
We were running a survey to the housefile and wanted to test the concept of introducing donor alignment and donor autonomy in treatment copy that would improve donor conversion. The control copy outlines the problem, gives urgency, and shares how the donor’s gift is a solution to the problem. But for the treatment, we developed a version that asks the donor to align themselves with specific beliefs in an attempt to get them to “nod their head” as they read along or give the opportunity for a step by step “micro yes” at each new paragraph on the page. We pose TGC’s beliefs and then ask the donor “Do you believe this as well?” We also ask a question that feeds off the momentum of the survey they just responded to: “If you believe TGC has made a positive impact on your faith and spiritual growth, would you consider giving back today?” Throughout the survey, the readers had a chance to tell us just how much value they had personally received from TGC resources. The donation page treatment introduces an opportunity for them to provide that same value to others. We open the donation page with a conversational question, “In today’s noise world what source can you trust for truth” as opposed to our control copy which leads with a statement “The digital world is a challenging place, full of unhelpful influences.” Rather than talking AT our donors, we made additional effort to talk TO them, and bring them into being a part of the mental conversation on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed was a 14.3% increase in conversion rate on the treatment with a 49.4% level of confidence so the test results are invalid. We did notice some directional increases and decreases for various audience segments: Returning visitors had a 40.0% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence. Email visitors had a 29.2% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence. 65+ visitors had an 18.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Finally, mobile visitors had a 67.7% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence. Because the confidence level for overall traffic is so low, we would need to re-run this test with more overt changes to be able to discern any true impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a landing page layout redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/15/2022
Through previous testing, we had found a winning version of the landing page but we identified a few changes we could implement to further optimize and test to achieve a higher conversion rate on the page. We developed a treatment that moved the eBook image to the right side of the page, relocated the supporting value proposition text/”reasons to download” from the bottom of the page into bullet points above the form, and added this copy leading into the form: “Tell us where to send your free copy now:”
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the experiment for one month and only achieved a 23.2% level of confidence that there was a 0.67% decrease in email acquisition on the page so the results are not valid. However, we did observe a valid increase in acquisition with some specific segments in the experiment that are worth noting: Email visitors had a 52.2% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. 45-54 aged visitors had an 18.3% increase in email acquisitions with a 96% level of confidence, and 55-64 aged visitors had a 34.6% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. Overall, what we learned was that putting the form lower on the page by increasing the text ahead of the call to action could have been causing an adverse effect on conversion for some visitors. For future testing, we will shorten the copy ahead of the form to see if the length of the copy is interrupting the experience and causing friction, and distracting from the intended call to action.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed giving form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2022 - 03/28/2022
When launching a new acquisition offer, we tested a tabbed giving form on the instant donation page. The existing form has a checkbox to make a recurring gift, but we were curious if visually presenting the option to choose between giving one-time or monthly would increase donor conversion. This concept has had varying results in previous tests with other organizations. First, we wanted to verify that this change would not have a negative impact on donor conversion before adding this feature to other donation pages. Secondly, we wanted to see how this would affect recurring donation conversion. All other copy and donation form elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for one month, but the results were ultimately inconclusive. We saw a 7.4% increase in conversion rate with a 29% level of confidence so the experiment results did not produce valid learning. We did, however, see some interesting observations on the impact of recurring donations. The control had 0 recurring gifts and the treatment saw 3 recurring donations. While this was too small of a sample size of transactions to be considered statistically significant, we will be able to use these results to replicate the experiment on additional acquisition pages knowing that it did not appear to have a negative impact on recurring donations based on the initial test. We did see some specific segments see a decrease in giving on the treatment that we will need to further explore on the next iteration of the test to see if similar tests produce similar results.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 41.9% decrease in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 33.8% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 50.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 14.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 7.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned calendar year-end donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for a calendar year-end donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2021 - 01/04/2022
Because we saw a lift in donor response in a previous experiment when adding relevant value proposition to a post-survey donation page, we wanted to introduce similar language on the calendar year-end donation page for TGC to capture the additional responses from email appeal traffic. In addition to outlining the value proposition in the body copy, we revised the headline to indicate how the donor’s gift would be used and mention the upcoming deadline. We also added a countdown clock at the top of the page to indicate urgency and added a live thermometer at the bottom of the page to show progress toward the revenue goal. Beneath the donation button, we added a sentence supporting a value proposition demonstrating the impact TGC donors have had throughout the year. Finally, we removed the navigation bar and alternated conflicting calls to action in the footer to keep the donor on the page and stay focused on moving forward with making their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we did not receive enough traffic to this particular page to validate the experiment. However, we did capture additional learnings by running this same test on the main donation page. Those learnings can be viewed in the write-up for that experiment which is linked here.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had an 80.6% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
